findings.summary: Secret management endpoints lack Cedar authorization checks
evidence: handle_put_secret, handle_delete_secret, handle_list_secrets have no Cedar auth checks
Similar tenant-scoped endpoints (policies, decisions) all require require_policy_auth
Secrets contain sensitive encrypted values and should have authorization gates
findings.suggestion: Add require_policy_auth or a dedicated manage_secrets authorization check to all secret management endpoints.

findings.summary: Health and metrics endpoints lack authorization checks unlike all other observe routes
evidence: handle_health (line 13) has no HeaderMap parameter at all, no auth check
handle_metrics (line 57) has no auth check, exposes detailed per-entity-type transition counts, invariant violations, and cross-invariant metrics
Every other /observe endpoint calls require_observe_auth
findings.suggestion: If these are intentionally public (monitoring probes), document them explicitly as public in the route registration and add a comment. If not, add require_observe_auth with read_metrics action. The metrics endpoint in particular leaks operational detail.

findings.summary: Several domain tables lack RLS: evolution_records, pending_decisions, tenant_policies, feature_requests
evidence: RLS coverage data shows without_rls includes: evolution_records, feature_requests, pending_decisions, tenant_policies
Sibling tables (events, specs, trajectories, wasm_modules) all have RLS enabled
These tables contain tenant-scoped data that could leak across tenants without RLS
findings.suggestion: Add RLS policies to evolution_records, pending_decisions, tenant_policies, and feature_requests tables. These store tenant-scoped data and should have the same RLS treatment as their siblings.

findings.summary: Deprecated temper_mcp::repl symbols are still actively imported by temper-server
evidence: temper-mcp/src/repl.rs marks both ReplConfig and run_repl as #[deprecated] with note 'Use temper_sandbox::repl directly'
temper-server/src/api/repl.rs (line 58, 62) still imports and calls temper_mcp::repl::ReplConfig and temper_mcp::repl::run_repl
findings.suggestion: Update temper-server/src/api/repl.rs to import directly from temper_sandbox::repl instead of the deprecated temper_mcp::repl shim. Then remove the deprecated shim module entirely.

findings.summary: Three deprecated methods in entity_ops.rs and dispatch/actions.rs remain defined but uncalled
evidence: get_or_spawn_actor (entity_ops.rs:74) is deprecated in favor of tenant-explicit variant, no callers found
get_entity_state (entity_ops.rs:273) is deprecated in favor of tenant-explicit variant, no callers found
dispatch (actions.rs:73) is deprecated in favor of DispatchCommand variant, no callers verified
findings.suggestion: Remove the deprecated methods since they have no callers. Keeping dead deprecated code increases maintenance burden and confuses onboarding engineers.

findings.summary: Dead function _legacy_postgres_path in trajectories.rs contains unreachable code
evidence: Line 80-83: fn _legacy_postgres_path() with #[allow(dead_code)] contains only 'if false {}' and a comment 'Removed: Postgres fallback path'
findings.suggestion: Delete the _legacy_postgres_path function entirely. It contains no logic and serves only as a historical marker that should be in version control history, not in the source.

findings.summary: 20 files have comment ratios 2-4x the codebase average (0.169), suggesting restating or formulaic comments
evidence: temper-agent-runtime/src/lib.rs has 0.62 comment ratio (3.7x codebase average)
temper-runtime/src/scheduler/sim_handler.rs has 0.53 comment ratio (3.1x average)
temper-observe/src/error.rs has 0.48 comment ratio -- every variant has a doc comment restating its name
temper-jit/src/table/mod.rs has 0.45 ratio on a 12-line file
sim_handler.rs doc comments like '/// Greater than.' on CompareOp::Gt and '/// Equal.' on CompareOp::Eq restate the variant name
findings.suggestion: Audit the 20 flagged files and remove doc comments that merely restate the symbol name (e.g., '/// Greater than.' on Gt, '/// Equal.' on Eq). Keep comments that explain WHY or non-obvious constraints. Focus on files with ratio > 0.40.

findings.summary: Schema module tests verify that constant arrays contain their literal elements
evidence: test_span_columns_has_required_fields asserts is_span_column('trace_id'), is_span_column('span_id') etc. -- these can never fail unless someone edits the constant, in which case the test also needs updating
Three identical-pattern tests for spans, logs, and metrics tables
These tests verify that an array literal contains the strings it was defined with -- pure tautology
findings.suggestion: Delete or convert these tests. If the intent is to prevent accidental removal of columns, a compile-time assertion or a single snapshot test would be more useful. These tautological tests add maintenance cost with no regression-detection value.

findings.summary: handle_feature_requests regenerates and upserts ALL feature requests from trajectories on every GET request
evidence: Lines 220-275: Every GET /observe/evolution/feature-requests first loads 10,000 trajectory entries, generates feature requests from them, upserts each one to Turso, creates FeatureRequest entities, THEN reads back from Turso with filter
This is an O(n) write-on-read side effect in what appears to be a pure list endpoint
The function signature and HTTP method (GET) suggest a read-only operation
findings.suggestion: Move feature request generation to the sentinel check endpoint (POST) or a background job. The GET endpoint should only read from Turso. Currently, listing feature requests is an expensive mutating operation disguised as a read.

findings.summary: operations.rs has 29 indirection chains at depth 4, highest in the codebase
evidence: Indirection hotspot analysis shows max_chain_depth=4, chain_count=29
Functions like persist_evolution_record -> turso.insert_evolution_record, then create_system_entity -> dispatch_tenant_action, called in sequence from each handler
Each handler (handle_sentinel_check, handle_feature_requests) chains persist_evolution_record + create_system_entity + persist_insights in deep call chains
findings.suggestion: Consider a unified EvolutionRecordWriter that combines the persist + system-entity-creation pattern into a single method. The current code duplicates this pattern across persist_alerts and persist_insights with slight variations.

findings.summary: handle_metrics repeats the same key-splitting and formatting pattern 6 times
evidence: Lines 62-178: Six blocks each read a map, split keys by ':', format into Prometheus exposition format
Each block is structurally identical: read lock, iterate, splitn(3, ':'), push formatted string
The pattern could be data-driven: a list of (metric_name, help_text, type, map_ref, label_names)
findings.suggestion: Extract a helper function like emit_prometheus_counter(lines, map, metric_name, help, labels) that takes the map reference and label names. This would reduce the 120 lines of repetitive formatting to ~30 lines of data declarations plus a ~15-line helper.

findings.summary: require_policy_auth returns Option<Response> where Some means denied -- inverted semantics
evidence: require_policy_auth returns Option<axum::response::Response> where None means authorized and Some(response) means denied
Every call site uses 'if let Some(resp) = require_policy_auth(...) { return resp; }' -- the double-negation pattern (if-some then return) is unusual
The sibling require_observe_auth returns Result<(), StatusCode> which is the standard pattern
findings.suggestion: Refactor require_policy_auth to return Result<(), Response> to match the require_observe_auth convention. Callers would use the standard '?' operator instead of the if-let-return pattern.

findings.summary: handle_update_feature_request passes empty string as disposition when not provided, not None
evidence: Line 342: turso.update_feature_request(&id, disposition.unwrap_or(''), notes)
An empty string disposition is semantically different from 'no update to disposition' but the Turso method receives it as a value to set
The function validates disposition if present (lines 327-335) but the empty-string fallback bypasses validation
findings.suggestion: Pass disposition as Option<&str> to the Turso method, or only call update when at least one field is provided. The current code may overwrite a valid disposition with an empty string if only notes are provided.

findings.summary: upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso wraps errors with anyhow::anyhow! while sibling function uses .context()
evidence: upsert_loaded_specs_to_postgres uses .with_context(|| format!(...))? (lines 56-77)
upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso uses .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!('Failed to...{e}'))? (lines 160-178)
The anyhow! pattern loses the original error's type and backtrace chain; .context() preserves them
findings.suggestion: Replace .map_err(|e| anyhow::anyhow!(...{e})) with .context('Failed to...')? in upsert_loaded_specs_to_turso to match the Postgres sibling and preserve error chains.

findings.summary: No integration tests verify authorization enforcement on observe or API endpoints
evidence: 10 integration test files in temper-server/tests/ cover OData, lifecycle, hotswap, persistence, multi-tenant, and WASM dispatch
None of the test files test authorization denial or the Cedar policy enforcement path
The authorization system is a critical cross-cutting concern with complex behavior (admin bypass, tenant scoping, denial recording)
findings.suggestion: Add integration tests that verify: (1) unauthenticated requests to observe endpoints are rejected, (2) cross-tenant requests without admin principal are denied, (3) decision approval generates correct Cedar policies.

findings.summary: record_invocation and invoke_and_handle_result accept 8+ parameters despite WasmDispatchCtx existing
evidence: invoke_and_handle_result (line 225) has #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)] with 8 params
record_invocation (called at lines 199, 253) takes 8 positional parameters
WasmDispatchCtx was created specifically to bundle related params, but these functions still receive unbundled arguments
findings.suggestion: Extend WasmDispatchCtx or create a WasmInvocationResult struct to bundle the invocation-specific parameters (module_name, hash, inv_ctx, host, limits, denial_tracker). This would bring these functions below the 5-param threshold.

findings.summary: Persistent store fallback pattern varies: some endpoints return empty, others return SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE
evidence: handle_feature_requests (operations.rs:306): returns empty JSON with 200 when no store
handle_update_feature_request (operations.rs:338): returns SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE when no store
handle_trajectories (trajectories.rs:69): returns empty JSON with 200 when no store
handle_list_decisions (decisions.rs:51): returns empty list with 200 when no store
handle_approve_decision (decisions.rs:93): returns SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE when no store
findings.suggestion: Establish a consistent pattern: read-only listing endpoints return empty collections (200), while mutating endpoints (update, approve, deny) return SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE. Document this convention and verify all handlers follow it.

findings.summary: The /api router directly calls handlers defined in the /observe module tree
evidence: api/mod.rs line 37: post(crate::observe::specs::handle_load_dir)
api/mod.rs line 44: post(crate::observe::wasm::upload_wasm_module)
api/mod.rs line 49: post(crate::observe::evolution::handle_decide)
The observe module is documented as 'read-only observation routes' (mod.rs line 136) but the api router mounts mutating endpoints from it
findings.suggestion: Move the mutating handlers (handle_load_dir, handle_load_inline, upload_wasm_module, delete_wasm_module, handle_decide, handle_unmet_intent, handle_sentinel_check) from the observe module into the api module. This would enforce the stated boundary that observe is read-only.

findings.summary: Decision handlers use impl IntoResponse while observe handlers use Result<Json<Value>, StatusCode>
evidence: handle_list_decisions returns impl IntoResponse
handle_approve_decision returns impl IntoResponse
handle_list_evolution_records returns Result<Json<Value>, StatusCode>
handle_list_specs returns Result<Json<Value>, StatusCode>
The two patterns have different error surface areas and make it harder to reason about what status codes an endpoint can return
findings.suggestion: Standardize on Result<Json<Value>, StatusCode> for endpoints that can only fail with a status code, or define a typed error enum. Reserve impl IntoResponse for endpoints that genuinely need mixed response types (e.g., SSE streams).

findings.summary: temper-server/src/ root has 13 files mixing concerns (events, idempotency, sentinel, query_eval)
evidence: 13 files at the crate root with total_loc=3309
event_store.rs, events.rs are SSE/event-sourcing related but sit alongside sentinel.rs (evolution domain)
query_eval.rs and idempotency.rs are cross-cutting infrastructure
The crate already has well-organized subdirectories (observe/, state/, entity_actor/) but these root files were not assigned
findings.suggestion: Group related root files into subdirectories: event_store.rs + events.rs into an events/ module, sentinel.rs into observe/evolution/ or a sentinel/ module, query_eval.rs into a query/ module. This would bring the root down to ~6-7 core files (lib.rs, router.rs, request_context.rs, response.rs, etc.).

findings.summary: Authorization actions and resource types are bare string literals scattered across handlers
evidence: require_observe_auth(&state, &headers, "read_specs", "Spec") in specs.rs
require_observe_auth(&state, &headers, "read_entities", "Entity") in entities.rs
require_observe_auth(&state, &headers, "read_evolution", "Evolution") in operations.rs
require_observe_auth(&state, &headers, "read_agents", "AgentAudit") in agents.rs
A typo in any action or resource string would silently fail open or closed with no compile-time check
findings.suggestion: Define constants or an enum for authorization actions (READ_SPECS, READ_ENTITIES, READ_EVOLUTION, etc.) and resource types (SPEC, ENTITY, EVOLUTION, etc.). This would prevent typos and make it easy to discover all permission checks.

findings.summary: temper-evolution RecordStore trait is deprecated but has no replacement trait defined
evidence: The RecordStore trait is marked #[deprecated(note = 'Use IOA entity dispatch to temper-system tenant (ADR-0025)')]
PostgresRecordStore still implements this trait and is actively used in CLI storage setup (connect_postgres_store)
The codebase convention is to use IOA entity dispatch, but the trait and its implementation remain as the active path for Postgres
findings.suggestion: Either complete the migration away from RecordStore (replacing PostgresRecordStore usage with entity dispatch) or remove the deprecated annotation if the migration is not planned. The current state creates ambiguity about which path is canonical.

findings.summary: OTEL initialization reads OTLP_ENDPOINT and LOGFIRE_TOKEN from environment at module init time
evidence: Line 70-71: std::env::var('OTLP_ENDPOINT') and std::env::var('LOGFIRE_TOKEN') are read during OTEL setup
Line 102: LOGFIRE_TOKEN is read again for header configuration
These are called from the CLI bootstrap path which is appropriate, but the function signature does not accept these as parameters
findings.suggestion: Accept the OTLP endpoint and Logfire token as function parameters to init_otel_tracing() rather than reading from environment inside the function. This improves testability and makes the configuration dependency explicit.

findings.summary: temper-evolution uses chrono::Utc::now() directly instead of sim_now() for record timestamps
evidence: records.rs line 441: let now = Utc::now() in RecordHeader::new()
chain.rs has 6 occurrences of chrono::Utc::now() for record timestamps
store.rs has 2 occurrences of chrono::Utc::now()
The project guidelines specify sim_now() for deterministic simulation, but temper-evolution is not in the simulation-visible crate list
However, evolution records are created during operations called from temper-server which IS simulation-visible
findings.suggestion: Evaluate whether temper-evolution should use sim_now() instead of Utc::now() for timestamps, since its record creation is triggered from simulation-visible server code. If wall-clock time is intentional here, add a // determinism-ok comment explaining why.

findings.summary: The observe/ directory contains mutating handlers that are mounted by the /api router
evidence: observe/specs/load_dir.rs and load_inline.rs contain spec-loading POST handlers used by api/mod.rs
observe/wasm.rs contains upload_wasm_module and delete_wasm_module POST/DELETE handlers used by api/mod.rs
observe/evolution/records_detail.rs contains handle_decide POST handler used by api/mod.rs
The observe module documentation says 'Read-only observation routes' but these files contain write operations
This creates a directory-to-responsibility mismatch
findings.suggestion: Move the mutating handler files (load_dir.rs, load_inline.rs, the upload/delete handlers from wasm.rs, and handle_decide from records_detail.rs) into the api/ directory. Keep the read-only observation handlers in observe/.
